tldr: Headlines across all 16 buckets identify Bonnie Tyler almost exclusively through 'Total Eclipse of the Heart', anchoring her death in pop-cultural legacy rather than medical narrative. Only four outlets (Bild, Daily Mail, Geo News, La Nación) surface the coma or surgical crisis in their titles — the health-safety framing that dominates body text is largely invisible at the homepage level.

frames.sub_frame: named by iconic song; 80s pop legacy

evidence.quote: Bonnie Tyler, 80s pop legend known for Total Eclipse
La chanteuse Bonnie Tyler, icône des années 80
la voz arenosa que reinó en los ochenta
cantora que eternizou 'Total Eclipse of the Heart'
who topped the charts with epic 'Total Eclipse of the Heart'
Morta Bonnie Tyler, la voce di "Total Eclipse of the Heart"
lideró los rankings con Eclipse total del corazón

frames.sub_frame: coma and surgery foregrounded in headline

evidence.quote: Bonnie Tyler (75) gestorben - Nach Krankheit und Koma
after emergency surgery which left her in a coma
passes away at 75 after concerning health update
De qué murió la cantante Bonnie Tyler

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only outlet to label Tyler a 'Rockstar' in the headline; every other outlet used 'singer,' 'Sängerin,' or 'cantora' — a genre-elevation absent from non-German press.

Only outlet to lead with a biographical question headline ('d'où lui venait cette voix rocailleuse'), framing the death as an occasion to interrogate the origin of her trademark voice rather than announce her passing.
One of two Le Parisien headlines uses 'icône des années 80' (80s icon) as the primary identity label — the only French title to anchor Tyler in era rather than song credential.

Only outlet to publish a cause-of-death inquiry as a standalone headline ('De qué murió la cantante Bonnie Tyler'), treating the medical circumstances as a separate editorial unit from the death announcement.
Only outlet to frame the headline as 'quem era' (who was she), shifting from death announcement to retrospective profile — a different editorial stance from all other Brazilian and global titles.
Only outlet to characterise Tyler's signature by her voice quality ('la voz arenosa') rather than naming a song — a critic's framing that foregrounds artistry over catalogue.

bottom_line: At the headline layer, Bonnie Tyler's death is universally framed as the loss of a pop-cultural icon defined by a single song; the two-month medical crisis that dominated body text is suppressed to near-invisibility in titles across 12 of 16 buckets.
